activities_projects.title: Stoichiometric Analysis of Compounds vs. Mixtures
activities_projects.objective: To quantitatively differentiate between a physical mixture and a chemically bonded compound[cite: 1232].
steps: Prepare two sets of iron and sulphur in a 5:3 ratio[cite: 1195].
Mix Set A thoroughly and test its magnetic properties and solubility in carbon disulphide[cite: 1184, 1202].
Heat Set B strongly until red hot, let it cool, and repeat the same tests[cite: 1199, 1202].
Observe the gas evolved when dilute sulphuric acid is added to both sets[cite: 1206].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Concludes that a compound has a fixed composition and totally different properties from its constituents[cite: 1225, 1228].
activities_projects.evaluation: Compare the properties of the gas obtained from Set A (Hydrogen) and Set B (Hydrogen Sulphide) [cite: 1208, 1209] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Solubility Dynamics and Temperature Coefficients
activities_projects.objective: To calculate the mass of solute required for saturation at specific temperatures[cite: 1042, 1300].
steps: Dissolve potassium nitrate in 100 g of water at 293 K and record the mass needed for saturation[cite: 1301].
Heat the solution to 313 K and determine the additional mass required to maintain saturation[cite: 1304].
Cool a saturated solution from 353 K to room temperature and observe the crystallization process[cite: 1305, 1306].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that different substances have different solubilities which increase with temperature[cite: 1047, 1312].
activities_projects.evaluation: Calculate the mass of potassium nitrate needed for a saturated solution in 50 g of water at 313 K [cite: 1304] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Tyndall Effect Quantification in Polydisperse Systems
activities_projects.objective: To analyze the relationship between particle size and light scattering (Tyndall Effect)[cite: 1087, 1116].
steps: Pass a fine beam of light through a true solution, a colloid, and a suspension[cite: 977, 1086].
Record the visibility of the light path in each medium[cite: 1026, 1105, 1106].
Observe the path visibility in a suspension before and after the particles settle down[cite: 1080].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies that colloids and suspensions scatter light due to particle sizes larger than 1 nm[cite: 1024, 1114].
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why the Tyndall effect is observed when sunlight passes through a dense forest canopy [cite: 1110, 1111] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Concentration Expression through Multi-Method Analysis
activities_projects.objective: To determine solution concentration using mass-by-mass and mass-by-volume metrics[cite: 1049].
steps: Dissolve 36 g of sodium chloride in 100 g of water at 293 K to reach the saturation point[cite: 1127].
Apply the mass-by-mass percentage formula: $(	ext{Mass of solute} / 	ext{Mass of solution}) 	imes 100$[cite: 1050, 1073].
Prepare a solution and express its concentration in mass-by-volume percentage[cite: 1058, 1060].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Mastery of quantitative methods for expressing the relative proportions of solute and solvent[cite: 1031, 1048].
activities_projects.evaluation: Calculate the concentration of a saturated salt solution prepared at 293 K [cite: 1127] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Mechanical Separation of Colloidal Systems
activities_projects.objective: To explore separation techniques for stable heterogeneous mixtures[cite: 1117, 1119].
steps: Attempt to separate milk particles using the standard filtration method[cite: 1118].
Subject the same sample to centrifugation[cite: 1119].
Compare the results and analyze why filtration fails for colloidal particles[cite: 1118].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Concludes that specialized techniques like centrifugation are required for particles that pass through filter paper[cite: 1119].
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain the principle of centrifugation used to separate butter from curd [cite: 1285] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Phase Identification in Complex Colloids
activities_projects.objective: To identify the dispersed phase and dispersing medium in various physical states[cite: 1120, 1122].
materials: Shaving cream, Fog, Sponge, Coloured gemstone
steps: Examine samples of different types of colloids (Aerosol, Foam, Emulsion, Sol, Gel)[cite: 1129].
Identify the dispersed phase (solute-like component) and dispersing medium for each[cite: 1121].
Determine the resulting colloid type based on the combination of states[cite: 1122, 1123].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Categorizes matter based on the physical state of its constituent phases[cite: 1123].
activities_projects.evaluation: Tabulate the dispersed phase and dispersing medium for 'Milk of Magnesia' and 'Cheese' [cite: 1129] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Thermodynamics of Chemical Reactions
activities_projects.objective: To differentiate chemical changes from physical interconversion of states[cite: 1137, 1141].
steps: Compare the inflammability of water and oil to identify their chemical characteristics[cite: 1139, 1140].
Perform the electrolysis of water to break it down into hydrogen and oxygen gases[cite: 1149].
Burn a magnesium ribbon and analyze the resulting substance[cite: 1143, 1144].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Learns that chemical reactions result in new substances with different chemical properties[cite: 1143].
activities_projects.evaluation: Is 'rusting of an almirah' a physical or chemical change? Justify based on composition [cite: 1148] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Metallurgical Property Profiling
activities_projects.objective: To investigate the physical properties that define metals, non-metals, and metalloids[cite: 1158, 1173].
materials: Copper, Iron, Sodium (Teacher only), Iodine, Silicon, Germanium
steps: Assess samples for metallic lustre, sonority, and malleability[cite: 1160, 1163, 1164].
Test electrical conductivity using a simple circuit[cite: 1161, 1167].
Identify elements with intermediate properties known as metalloids[cite: 1173, 1174].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Differentiates elements based on their ability to conduct, their physical strength, and appearance[cite: 1159, 1166].
activities_projects.evaluation: Identify the only metal that remains liquid at room temperature [cite: 1165] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Purification Verification of Colourless Liquids
activities_projects.objective: To establish experimental criteria for confirming the purity of a substance[cite: 941, 1325].
steps: Measure the boiling point of the given colourless liquid[cite: 1136].
Compare it to the standard boiling point of pure water (373 K)[cite: 708, 1136].
Perform evaporation to check for any solid residue[cite: 945, 1279].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that pure substances have fixed physical constants like boiling points[cite: 1136, 1225].
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain how you would confirm that a colourless liquid is pure water [cite: 1325] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Multi-Step Separation of Complex Mixtures
activities_projects.objective: To design a logical sequence of separation techniques for multi-component systems[cite: 1263, 1276].
materials: Mixture of Sand, Iron pins, Ammonium chloride, and Common salt
steps: Use a magnet to remove iron pins[cite: 1289].
Apply sublimation to separate ammonium chloride[cite: 1280].
Dissolve the residue in water, filter the sand, and evaporate the water to obtain salt[cite: 1279, 1287].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Mastery of physical separation processes based on the unique properties of mixture components[cite: 1230, 1263].
activities_projects.evaluation: Which technique would you apply to separate oil from water? [cite: 1286] (10 marks).
